issues.prompt: Document your personal system for systematically checking a bicycle when a rider reports "something doesn't feel right."
Reflect on what you hope readers will think about or feel differently after reading your book.
Document your evolving relationship with perfectionism and note specific areas where you're learning to embrace "good enough."
Explore how perfectionism shows up in your daily life and what triggers your need to be "good enough."
Reflect on how birth order, family crises, or significant losses shaped your role as the "helper," "peacekeeper," or "problem-solver."
Reflect on how you prioritize multiple performance issues when a user reports their computer is "running slow."
Reflect on the difference between content you actively reference versus content you're just hoarding "just in case."
Document your answers to the question: "What would I do with my time if I had no digital distractions for an entire week?"
Write down trusted people who could help in a crisis and think about how to contact them if your usual methods aren't available.
Write about the differences you notice between "good mental health days" and "challenging mental health days."
Study the concept of emotional granularity and practice naming your emotions with more specific vocabulary than "good" or "bad."
Note any physical sensations or emotions that arise when you think about your current financial situation.
Document one financial concept you recently learned that changed how you think about money.
List the new safety considerations you need to think about when grandchildren visit your home.
Research and document the science or theory behind techniques you use, understanding the "why" behind the "how."
Research the precise legal meanings of commonly misunderstood terms like "shall," "may," "covenant," and "warranty."
Research how successful people in your field think about setbacks and compile their approaches to resilience.
Reflect on which emotions (anger, fear, frustration, sadness) are most prominent when you think about this dispute.
Collect examples of inclusive language alternatives to gendered terms like "ladies and gentlemen" or "boyfriend/girlfriend."
Note which accounts are connected to social media platforms or use social login features like "Sign in with Google" or "Sign in with Facebook."
Reflect on the earliest memories you have of feeling like your efforts weren't "good enough."
Research and note the key differences between how wealthy people and poor people think about money according to financial psychology studies.
Document the difference between how you think about tasks when you're motivated versus when you're procrastinating.
Reflect on your understanding of legal terms like "equitable distribution," "community property," and "separate property."
Note any recurring thoughts or worries that surface when you think about the future.
Research the historical origins and cultural contexts of spiritual practices you were taught were "timeless" or "universal."
Analyze your bank statements from the past three months and categorize purchases into "needs," "wants," and "impulse buys."
Explore the connection between perfectionism and skin picking by noting when you feel things must be "just right."
Think about a time when you misunderstood someone's tone and write down what you learned from that experience.
Reflect on how learning new vocabulary has changed the way you think about concepts or express ideas.
Think about and document any specific items or heirlooms you want particular people to receive and why.
Practice describing wine textures and mouthfeel sensations, creating your own vocabulary list for terms like "silky," "tannic," or "creamy."
List and describe the physical sensations you experience in your body when you think about taking a complete break from work.
